---
title: "Air Traffic On-Time Performance Analysis"
---

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## 📄 Read the Full Report

The complete project report is available as a PDF.

<a class="btn btn-primary btn-lg" href="air.pdf">Read in browser</a>
&nbsp;
<a class="btn btn-outline-primary btn-lg" href="air.pdf" download>⬇ Download PDF</a>
:::

## Overview

This project examines U.S. domestic airline on-time performance data from September 2019
using MySQL for data querying and R for visualization. The analysis covers three major topics:
airline and airport departure delay patterns, weekly flight volume trends, and flight
cancellation reasons.

On-time performance data contains records of flights by date, airline, originating airport,
destination airport, and many other operational details. The goal is to identify which airlines
and airports perform poorly, which days of the week are busiest, and what drives flight
cancellations.

## Abstract

Numerous flights operate every day across hundreds of destinations in the United States,
and unexpected schedule changes occur for a wide variety of reasons — weather events,
carrier issues, and national air system disruptions. With real-time flight data from
September 1–30, 2019, this project examines a dataset of 605,979 flights across 17 airlines
and 285 airports to answer key operational questions about airline performance.

The first topic is airline and airport departure analysis. Airlines vary significantly in
their worst-case delay performance, with Envoy Air recording a maximum departure delay of
1,753 minutes and Southwest Airlines recording the shortest at 438 minutes. Airport-level
analysis reveals that Southwest Oregon Regional Airport had the highest average departure
delay at 43.92 minutes.

The second topic is weekly flight performance analysis. Monday was the busiest day of the
week with 106,461 flights, while Saturday had the fewest at 67,034. A rolling three-day
flight count analysis shows a repeating five-day cycle, with Wednesdays consistently
producing the highest cumulative totals due to the preceding Sunday and Monday peaks.

The third topic is cancellation reason analysis. Of 605,979 total flights, 10,016 were
cancelled — a rate of 1.7%. Weather was the dominant cancellation cause at 86.2%, consistent
with September being peak hurricane season in the United States. The most-affected airports
are concentrated in the southern states, where severe weather conditions are most prevalent
during this period.
